rationale: The citing paper uses Hoeffler (2003) multiple times in its core theory section, not just in a background list: it cites it as key prior evidence on really new products and on the role of visualization in RNP preference formation. The authors explicitly position their contribution as explaining when visualization helps or hurts by distinguishing imagination-focused versus memory-focused visualization. This prior work helps motivate hypotheses, interpret pretest differences between RNPs and INPs, and frame the paper's contribution, indicating a meaningful functional role.
relation_description: The citing paper extends Hoeffler (2003) by building on its RNP visualization line of work and introducing a new distinction in visualization focus (imagination vs memory). It moves from prior evidence on preference measurement/stability to testing effects on product evaluation and the mediating role of perceived benefit value.
